articles.headline: Extract from Benjamin F. Hughes' Eulogium on the Character of Wilberforce (continued)
articles.author: Benjamin F. Hughes
articles.summary: Continuation of Hughes's eulogy on William Wilberforce, recounting his 1789-1807 Parliamentary campaign against the British slave trade, quoting his 1791 speech and detailing opposition from pro-slavery interests before the trade's 1807 abolition.

articles.headline: Slander Refuted -- Benjamin Banneker's letter to Thomas Jefferson (1792) and Jefferson's reply
articles.summary: Full reprint, from Zion's Watchman, of Benjamin Banneker's August 19, 1792 letter to Thomas Jefferson protesting racial prejudice and enclosing a manuscript almanac, and Jefferson's August 30 reply praising Banneker's talents. Introduced by a correspondent signed 'N. B. A.' as evidence against claims that Africans cannot rise in intelligence.

articles.headline: Methodist and Zion Conferences -- schedule and comparative history
articles.summary: Report on the upcoming Bethel Methodist (Rt. Rev. Morris Brown, Bishop) and Zion Methodist (Rev. Christopher Rush, Superintendent) annual conferences; describes the Bethel Connexion's four conferences (Baltimore, Philadelphia, New York, Ohio) and Zion's two, plus a separate 'Union Methodist Connexion' under Bishop Peter Spencer of Wilmington, Delaware.

articles.headline: Pittsburgh Temperance Society report praised; Vashon, Woodson, Bryan named
articles.summary: Editorial remarks praising the Pittsburgh colored Temperance Society's annual report and naming John B. Vashon, Lewis Woodson, and 'Bryan' as leading, capable men of that city; reprints Pittsburgh society officers' language on total abstinence.

articles.headline: Missing girl notice: Frances Maria Shields
articles.author: D. Ruggles
articles.summary: Notice for Frances Maria Shields, about 12 years old, missing since Monday May 15; description of dress given; information to be left with D. Ruggles, Secretary of the New York Committee of Vigilance, or at several named addresses.

articles.headline: Missing person notice: George Nutt
articles.summary: Notice that George Nutt, a colored young man about 22, has been missing since mid-April after leaving his employment saying he intended to board a steamboat; his afflicted father seeks information.

articles.headline: Proposals and Plan of the Colored American, with founding endorsement
articles.author: Samuel E. Cornish
articles.summary: Reprint of the paper's founding prospectus and the endorsement signed by Arthur Tappan, Joshua Leavitt, Lewis Tappan, Simeon S. Jocelyn, R. G. Williams, E. Wright Jr., Gerrit Smith, Peter Williams, A. A. Phelps, and Theo. S. Wright.

articles.headline: Extract from Benjamin F. Hughes' Eulogium on Wilberforce (conclusion)
articles.author: Benjamin F. Hughes
articles.summary: Conclusion of the Wilberforce eulogy, comparing him favorably to classical statesmen, describing his Christian character, his 1823 'Protest' against the American Colonization Society, and his death and funeral honors alongside Pitt, Fox, and Canning.

articles.headline: Important Testimony -- 1828 Virginia Anti-Slavery Convention address reprinted
articles.author: Henry C. Wright
articles.summary: Henry C. Wright forwards an 1828 address of the Virginia Convention for the Abolition of Slavery to the people of Virginia (drafted by a committee of Benjamin F. Taylor, Robert Bond, Daniel Janney, Christian Holmes; signed Joseph Hockney, President, Thomas Wright, Secretary), arguing slavery's economic harm to Virginia versus free-labor Ohio and Massachusetts.

articles.headline: Two Sins of the Church -- pew rents and prejudice against color
articles.summary: Essay denouncing two church sins: expensive pew-rent systems that exclude the poor, and racial prejudice, illustrated by two anecdotes -- a Quaker meeting family excluded from full membership despite lifelong attendance, and a Methodist quarterly meeting near Philadelphia where Black congregants were seated outdoors under oak trees while white members kept the shaded pews.

articles.headline: Jamaica, Long Island -- people of color's meeting, Juvenile Benevolent Society formed
articles.summary: Meeting of the people of color of the Township of Jamaica, Queen's County, NY, May 10 1837, Wilson Rantous in the Chair and Samuel V. Berry Secretary; resolutions adopted and a committee appointed to draft a constitution for a benevolent society; continued at an adjourned meeting May 12 where the 'Jamaica Benevolent Society' constitution was adopted and officers elected.

articles.headline: John Davis freed from kidnappers Boudinot and Nash by church collections
articles.summary: Report that John Davis, arrested weeks earlier by 'negro catchers' Boudinot and Nash, was freed by payment of about $300 raised through contributions and collections in several New York churches; editorial expresses general opposition to paying off kidnappers but notes Davis's respectable character.

articles.headline: Caution against C. W. Carnes, mulatto impostor
articles.summary: Warning to colored readers in New York and elsewhere against trusting a man calling himself C. W. Carnes, late of Boston, a mulatto who sometimes passes for a Spaniard.

articles.headline: Historical Evidence Concerning the Effects of Immediate Emancipation
articles.summary: Extended survey arguing immediate slave emancipation caused no disorder, citing St. Domingo (1793), Guadaloupe (1794), Chile, Colombia, Mexico, the Cape Colony Hottentots (1823), and the British West Indies emancipation of August 1, 1834, with testimony from missionaries and colonial officials including the Marquis of Sligo.

articles.headline: Slavery in the Church
articles.summary: A correspondent describes hearing 'Mr. Garnet' (a colored man, evidently Henry Highland Garnet) preach powerfully at the recent American Anti-Slavery Society meeting, contrasting his plain eloquence with hollow ministerial rhetoric, and criticizing ministers who avoid direct denunciation of slavery from the pulpit.

articles.headline: Book notices: 'Negro Pew,' Mott's Biographical Sketches, Easton's Treatise
articles.summary: Notices of three books: 'Negro Pew' (anonymous treatise against church seating discrimination by color, preface reprinted at length), the second edition of Mott's 'Biographical Sketches and Interesting Anecdotes of Persons of Color,' and Rev. H. Easton's 'Treatise on the Intellectual Character, and Civil and Moral Condition of the Colored People of the United States.'

articles.headline: Prejudice Against Color -- essay
articles.summary: Essay arguing that prejudice against color, not color itself, is the root of American slavery and caste, citing scriptural and scientific reasoning (including a claim attributed to Blumenbach on climate and pigmentation) to argue skin color carries no inherent demerit.

articles.headline: New-York Young Men's General Institution proposed
articles.author: John P. Burr and Stephen H. Gloucester
articles.summary: Call, signed by John P. Burr (Chairman) and Stephen H. Gloucester (Secretary) on behalf of a Board, to form a general benevolent and improvement institution for young colored men of New York, ages 14-35, modeled on similar associations elsewhere.

articles.headline: Marriages: Rev. Theodore S. Wright to Adaline T. Turpin; Richard D. Williams to Anna P. Harris
articles.summary: Married at New Rochelle May 29 by Rev. S. E. Cornish: Rev. Theodore S. Wright, pastor of the First Colored Presbyterian Church, New York, to Adaline T. Turpin. Also, May 23 in New York, by Rev. Theo. S. Wright: Richard D. Williams of Philadelphia to Anna P. Harris of Schenectady, NY.

articles.headline: Death of Rev. Henry Drayton
articles.summary: Death notice, May 26, of the Rev. Henry Drayton of the Methodist Episcopal Church, aged 59 years.

articles.headline: American Moral Reform Society -- first anniversary announced
articles.summary: Notice that the American Moral Reform Society will hold its first anniversary in Philadelphia on the second Monday of August; auxiliary societies invited to send delegates; addresses expected on education, temperance, economy, peace, and universal liberty.

articles.headline: Address before the Female Branch Society of Zion
articles.author: Wm. Thompson
articles.summary: Address delivered by Wm. Thompson at Zion's Church, April 8, before the Female Branch Society of Zion, praising the moral influence of Black women's benevolent societies in New York including the Female Benevolent (Mother) Society, the Mite Society, and citing the newly established Colored American as an extension of collective self-improvement.

articles.headline: Resolutions of the American Anti-Slavery Society's Fourth Anniversary
articles.summary: Resolutions adopted by the American Anti-Slavery Society at its May 1837 anniversary meeting: condemning the Texas revolution and annexation designs as pro-slavery; calling for U.S. recognition of Haitian independence; urging a July 4th/August 1st fundraising day; condemning pro-slavery ecclesiastical bodies including the Methodist Conferences; and praising Thaddeus Stevens's stand against a pro-slavery convention in Harrisburg, PA.

articles.headline: Resolutions of the Lowell (MA) Quarterly Methodist Conference
articles.author: D. Kilbury, P.E.
articles.summary: Resolutions unanimously adopted by the Quarterly Methodist Conference of Lowell Station condemning American slavery as sin, calling on Methodist annual and general conferences to testify against slavery.

articles.headline: Free colored settlement of Smith Township, Columbiana County, Ohio -- statistics
articles.summary: Letter reprinted describing a sixteen-year-old free Black settlement extending into Stark County, Ohio: 264 persons, 100 church members, 4 colored preachers, 14 formerly enslaved (who paid about $3,000 for their freedom), and property totaling $39,497 (1,860 acres of land, livestock, and other property), cited as refutation of claims that colored people cannot support themselves. Job Johnson of Mount Union and George Gonzales named as sources of the information.

articles.headline: Colored man ejected from a Broadway omnibus -- editorial rebuke
articles.summary: Reprint from the Times of an incident in which an omnibus driver refused a Black passenger and threatened him with a whip; editorial rebukes the driver, the newspaper, and onlookers, and urges the insulted man to seek legal redress.

articles.headline: Colored people's views on Colonization -- N. R. Pierson correspondence
articles.author: N. R. Pierson
articles.summary: Correspondent reports canvassing multiple Black church members on their views of the American Colonization Society plan versus abolition, finding uniform opposition to colonization; quotes an elderly woman ('we have worn ourselves out for the benefit of them...we have a right to this country') and other members.

articles.headline: Philadelphia meeting at Bethel Church opposing disenfranchisement -- Bishop Morris Brown chairs
articles.author: Wm. Yates
articles.summary: Letter dated Philadelphia, June 6, 1837, reporting a large meeting at Bethel Church called after news that the Pennsylvania Constitutional Convention at Harrisburg was considering stripping Black citizens of the right of suffrage they had held for 49 years. Bishop Morris Brown chaired; Frederick A. Hinton and Rev. Mr. Boggs assisted the Chair; James Cornish and James Needham were secretaries. Frederick A. Hinton and Charles Gardner were chosen as delegates to carry a memorial to Harrisburg and to seek to be heard before the Convention.

articles.headline: Death of Mr. William Louder
articles.summary: Death notice for Mr. William Louder of New York, aged 55, who died May 27; noted for bearing his illness with Christian fortitude.

articles.headline: Declaration of the American Anti-Slavery Society's principles ('The sin of slavery')
articles.summary: Extended doctrinal declaration on the sinfulness of slavery, its effects on slaveholders and the church, the meaning of immediate emancipation, and the Society's planned methods (agents, tracts, petitions to Congress, free-produce preference), concluding with a solemn subscription of principles.

articles.headline: Samuel E. Cornish's account of 16 years' exclusion from the Presbyterian General Assembly
articles.author: Samuel E. Cornish
articles.summary: Cornish recounts that since joining the Presbytery of New York in 1821, every other minister -- including newly arrived foreign-born clergy -- has in rotation been sent as a delegate to the Presbyterian General Assembly, except himself, attributing this 16-year exclusion to racial prejudice within the church; criticizes the General Assembly's broader accommodation of slaveholding.

articles.headline: Pennsylvania Constitutional Convention -- proposal to disfranchise colored citizens denounced
articles.summary: Editorial condemning a proposition introduced at the Pennsylvania Constitutional Convention at Harrisburg to disfranchise the state's Black citizens, arguing it would be an anomaly for a convention of 'republicans' to strip rights from any class of citizens.

articles.headline: A Colored Baltimorean on the deficiencies of the colored ministry
articles.author: A Colored Baltimorean
articles.summary: Letter from Baltimore, June 8 1837, arguing that many colored clergy lack sufficient education due to having been enslaved or otherwise denied schooling, criticizing anti-intellectual currents among some colored ministers who disparage learning, commentaries, and prepared notes.

articles.headline: New Jersey fugitive slave law -- full text (supplement, passed Feb 15, 1837)
articles.summary: Full text of a New Jersey supplementary act concerning fugitive slaves, establishing procedures for hearings before judges of the court of common pleas, jury trials on demand, fees, and penalties for judges who improperly certify removal of a fugitive.

articles.headline: New York City Mayor's address on the condition of the city's colored poor
articles.summary: Reprinted excerpt from the New York Mayor's address to the Common Council describing several thousand colored New Yorkers, many freed by Daniel D. Tompkins, being displaced by immigrant competition into deteriorating housing while denied relief given to immigrant paupers.

articles.headline: Obituary of Mrs. Margaretta Van Stay
articles.author: C. B. R.
articles.summary: Detailed obituary of Mrs. Margaretta Van Stay, consort of Marinus Van Stay, who died June 8 1837 aged 20, describing her deathbed religious conversion and final composure; signed C.B.R. (likely Charles B. Ray).

events.name: Jamaica, Long Island people of color's meeting -- Juvenile Benevolent Society founded
events.location: Colored Public School, Washington Street, Jamaica, Queen's County, NY (Long Island)
events.description: Meeting of the free people of color of the Township of Jamaica; Wilson Rantous called to the Chair, Samuel V. Berry appointed Secretary; committee appointed to draft a constitution. Adjourned meeting May 12 adopted the constitution under the name 'The Juvenile Benevolent Society' and elected officers.

events.name: John Davis freed from kidnappers Boudinot and Nash
events.description: John Davis, arrested weeks earlier by professional 'negro catchers' Boudinot and Nash, was released after about $300 was raised through contributions and collections at several New York churches.

events.name: New-York Young Men's General Institution -- proposed benevolent society
events.description: Call, on behalf of a Board, for the young colored men of New York (ages 14-35) to form a general benevolent and mutual-improvement institution, modeled on similar bodies elsewhere; signed by the Board's Chairman and Secretary.

events.name: Philadelphia mass meeting at Bethel Church against Pennsylvania disenfranchisement
events.description: Large public meeting called on short notice after news that the Pennsylvania Constitutional Convention sitting at Harrisburg was considering a clause to strip Black Pennsylvanians of the suffrage right they had held since 1790. Bishop Morris Brown chaired; Frederick A. Hinton presented resolutions and a preamble directing preparation of a memorial documenting the moral, social and religious condition of Philadelphia's Black community, to rebut the 'degradation' rationale for disenfranchisement; a committee of thirty was appointed, and Frederick A. Hinton and Charles Gardner were chosen as delegates to carry the memorial to Harrisburg and seek to be heard before the Convention.
attendees: assistant to the Chair; offered resolutions; delegate to Harrisburg

events.name: Free Black settlement of Smith Township / Stark County, Ohio -- described in correspondence
events.location: Smith Township, Columbiana County, Ohio (extending into Stark County)
events.description: A free Black agricultural settlement founded about 1821 (sixteen years before this report), comprising 264 persons with a church, two schoolhouses, a 120-volume library, no recorded fighting or quarreling, and collective property (land, livestock, other) valued at $39,497 -- cited to refute claims that free Black people cannot support themselves.
attendees: Mount Union resident who compiled the statistics
